results.possibleIdentity: Thomas Lamb (1938–1997) was a prominent Canadian industrial designer based in Toronto, Ontario. He was the founder of the firm Thomas Lamb & Associates, Limited, which was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s and later became known as Ambiant Systems Ltd. Lamb is best known for his furniture designs that utilized steam-bent wood and molded plywood techniques, reflecting a 'Scandinavian' influence on Canadian modernism. His most celebrated work is the 'Steamer' lounge chair (designed around 1978), which in 1979 became the first piece of Canadian design to enter the permanent collection of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York. Other notable designs include the 'Roo' chair (1970) and various office and institutional furniture systems. In 1982, he was appointed as the design director and principal designer for the high-end furniture manufacturer Nienkämper. His professional records, including drawings and corporate papers, are held in the Archives of Ontario as part of the Thomas Lamb fonds. His inclusion in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) registry suggests he was one of the technical professionals interested in interdisciplinary collaborations between the arts and technology during the organization's active period in Toronto.
